metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Components - Chips

header.title: Chip
header.description: Chips allow users to view information, make selections, filter content, and enter data

items: There are four types of chips: input, assist, filter, and suggestion

Assist, filter, and suggestion chips offer two styles: outlined and elevated. By default, outlined chips should be used. Elevated chips are preferred when used over a dynamic background like an image or video
Use chips to provide additional actions contextual to the content
Chips should not be used as an alternative to buttons
Always use the provided Atmosphere Design System Icons Library

data.resource: Figma: Atmosphere Foundations and Components Library

sections.textBefore:
Types

Chips fall into the following four types. Refer to the usage section in guidelines for their appropriate use cases.

Styles

Input chips come in an outlined style, while assist chips, filter chips, and suggestion chips come in both outlined and elevated styles. Refer to the usage section in guidelines for their appropriate use cases.

sections.text:
Measurements

The chip component comes in two density modes: relaxed and condensed. By default, it's recommended to use the relaxed density mode for improved accessibility. However, the condensed density mode can be beneficial when presenting a large amount of information on a larger screen. It's advisable to avoid the use of condensed density mode when designing applications for small & medium viewports.

sections.textBefore:
Usage

Chips are interactive components that can be used to enter information, make selections, filter content, or trigger actions.
sections.textAfter:
Below are some of its usage characteristics:

  • Chips shouldn’t be used in place of buttons. Buttons are consistently used for more prominent actions like Save, Confirm, or Add to Cart. Chips are better suited for dynamic use cases where additional interactions are required to encourage action.
  • Multiple chips can be grouped together to form a set of interactive elements that offer additional actions.
  • Chip sets can be scrolled horizontally.
  • The type of chip should be chosen based on its purpose and author. For example, if a chip represents an action, use an assist chip or filter chip. If the content is generated by a product or a person using the product, use a suggestion chip or input chip.

sections.text:
Input chip

Usage guidelines:

  • Input chips represent discrete pieces of information entered by a user, such as email contacts or filter options within a search field.
  • They enable user input and verify it by converting text into chips.
  • Input chips support editing, allowing users to change their contents.
  • A single field can contain multiple input chips, which can be reordered or moved into other fields.
  • Input chips can be removed by clicking on the close button within the chip.

Assist chips

Usage guidelines:

  • Assist chips are similar to buttons but can be used dynamically and contextually in a UI.
  • They can trigger actions or show progress and confirmation.
  • They can be used to provide additional contextual actions in a UI.

Filter chips

Usage guidelines:

  • Filter chips use tags or descriptive words to filter content.
  • Clicking on a filter chip activates it and adds a checkmark icon to the start of the chip label.
  • Multiple filter chips can be selected or unselected, offering a good alternative to checkboxes when viewing a list or search results.
  • Filter chips can also be selected individually, providing an alternative to radio buttons or single-select menus.
  • Single-select and multi-select behavior should not be mixed within a page.
  • Filter chips can also be removed by clicking on the close button within the chip.

Suggestion chips

Usage guidelines:

  • Suggestion chips help narrow a user’s intent by presenting dynamically generated suggestions, such as possible responses or search filters.
  • Suggestion chips can be used in a form field or search field to pre-fill the content.

sections.textAfter:
1. Container
  2. Trailing icon (optional)
  3. Label text
  4. Remove button (optional)
  5. Avatar
  6. Favicon or image (optional)
  7. Leading icon (optional)

sections.text:
Container

Chips always use a container to hold the elements inside.

Chips are transparent by default, but chip containers can be elevated if placed on top of an image or dynamic background.

Label text

The label text should be concise, using as few words as possible.

The character count of chip label text should not exceed 20 characters. Label text longer than 20 characters should be truncated.

The first letter of the label text should be capitalized.

Leading icon, favicon or avatar (optional)

Chips can contain a leading icon, favicon, or avatar.

The leading avatar can display the user's image or show the first letter of their first name if no image is available. The avatar changes to a checkmark when the chip is selected.

Trailing icon or remove button (optional)

The trailing icon can be used to open a Menu.

In medium, large, and extra-large viewports, Input chips and filter chips can include a remove button to directly delete the chip.

Placement

When displaying multiple chips together, arrange them inline as a row of options rather than vertically listed. Overflowing chips should break to the next line.

If the field is only one row high, chip sets can scroll horizontally.

Text labels of the chips should be truncated when used inside a wrapped container and the width of the chip exceeds the container width.

Maintain a minimum margin of 8px between chips when stacked in a grid format.

Use cases

Users should be able to:

  • Use a chip to perform an action
  • Navigate to a chip using assistive technology
  • Activate a chip with keyboard or switch input

Cursor

Users can activate a chip by clicking anywhere inside the chip container. The cursor will change to a hand cursor when hovered over and pressed.

When the chip is disabled, the cursor will not change when hovering or pressing on it, and there will be no interaction.

Keyboard

Users should be able to navigate to and select an active chip using the specified keys.

Labeling elements

The accessibility label for a chip is the chip's label text. Additional actions, like select or remove, are labelled separately.

Multi-select

Users can activate a chip by clicking anywhere inside the chip container. The cursor will change to a hand cursor when hovered over and pressed.

When the chip is disabled, the cursor will not change when hovering or pressing on it, and there will be no interaction.

Drop down list

Users can activate a chip by clicking anywhere inside the chip container. The cursor will change to a hand cursor when hovered over and pressed.

When the chip is disabled, the cursor will not change when hovering or pressing on it, and there will be no interaction.

Input chip remove action

Users can activate a chip by clicking anywhere inside the chip container. The cursor will change to a hand cursor when hovered over and pressed.

When the chip is disabled, the cursor will not change when hovering or pressing on it, and there will be no interaction.
